specialism.name: docstrings
specialism.task: Add or normalize Julia docstrings on public symbols (exported types, functions, and constants) across the package source. The goal is complete, consistent API documentation that renders correctly under Documenter.jl and follows whichever docstring convention is already established in the package (often DocStringExtensions macros such as $(TYPEDEF), $(TYPEDFIELDS), $(TYPEDSIGNATURES)). Output from this specialism makes the package's public API fully self-documenting and satisfies any checkdocs requirement in the docs build.

workflow.step:
Detect the existing convention. Read 1–2 symbols that already have complete docstrings to calibrate style: identify whether DocStringExtensions macros are used, how prose is structured relative to macro-generated content, and what field documentation pattern is preferred (inline comment strings above fields vs. separate docstrings). This detected baseline becomes the style target for all new or normalized docstrings.
In this package the established struct convention is: — `# Fields` heading on its own line, blank line, then `$(TYPEDFIELDS)`. — When constructor overloads exist: `# Generic constructor` (or `# Other constructors:`) heading followed by the signature verbatim, then `$(METHODLIST)` (see `src/EnsembleKalmanProcess.jl:156–199`). The established function convention is: `$(TYPEDSIGNATURES)` first, one prose paragraph, then an `Inputs:` line followed by a bullet list — not a `# Arguments` section.

Enumerate public symbols missing or incomplete docstrings. Run: grep -nE '^(function |struct |abstract type |mutable struct |const )' src/**/*.jl Cross-reference the result with the module's exported names (check the main module file for export statements). For each exported symbol, check whether a non-empty docstring precedes the definition. Produce a prioritized list: missing first, empty/stub second, incomplete third.

For each candidate, draft a docstring matching the detected convention: - Use the same macro set as existing documented symbols. - Preserve any inline field comment strings already present above struct fields. - Prose should answer "what does this represent / do, when would I use it, what are the units of key fields". Do not duplicate what the macros generate (e.g. do not restate field types if $(TYPEDFIELDS) already renders them). - Physical quantities: include units in square brackets, e.g. [m/day]. - For functions with more than two arguments, or whose argument semantics are not obvious from the name alone, add a `# Arguments` section listing each parameter as `- \`name\`: description [unit if applicable]`. - For every non-trivial public function where a minimal runnable example can be written, consider adding a `# Examples` section containing a `jldoctest` block — this is aspirational; the package currently contains no `jldoctest` blocks, so do not add one unless the user explicitly requests it or the function is simple enough that the test is trivially maintainable.

Verify: confirm the package loads without error: julia --project -e 'using <PackageName>' If a docs build is configured (make.jl present), run it and resolve any checkdocs warnings introduced by the new docstrings.

rubric.criterion: Every exported symbol has a non-empty docstring after the task is applied.
New docstrings use the same macro set and structural pattern as the best-documented symbols already in the package.

Prose answers "what, when, why" — not just restates the symbol name. Multi-argument or non-obvious functions include an `Inputs:` bullet list (or `# Arguments` section). Units are present in brackets for any physical quantity fields. `jldoctest` blocks are aspirational; do not count their absence as a deficiency unless the user requested them.

Prose does not duplicate content that DocStringExtensions macros generate automatically (field lists, method signatures). Field inline comment strings do not restate the field's type ($(TYPEDFIELDS) already renders it); square-bracket notation in field comments is reserved for physical units only.
Package loads without error; if docs build is configured, it completes without new warnings.

formatting.rule: Use triple-quoted strings for all docstrings.
First line: concise one-line summary (imperative mood for functions, noun phrase for types).
Remaining lines: prose, then macro invocations ($(TYPEDSIGNATURES) before body if used).
No trailing whitespace on any line inside the docstring.
Physical units in square brackets: [m/day], [kg/m³], etc.

Struct docstrings use explicit section headers: `# Fields` (blank line, then `$(TYPEDFIELDS)`) and, when constructor variants exist, `# Generic constructor` (signature verbatim) followed by `# Other constructors:` then `$(METHODLIST)`. Do not emit bare macro invocations without these headers.
Struct field inline comment strings (the string literal above each field) are distinct from the struct-level docstring — preserve both; do not merge them.

Field comment strings must describe the field's semantic role, not its type. Never write a type name inside brackets (e.g. "[Date]", "[Dict]", "[DataFrame]") — $(TYPEDFIELDS) already renders the type. Reserve square-bracket notation exclusively for physical units (e.g. [m/day], [day], [m³/s]).

Avoid vague labels such as "data object" or "container" — say what the field represents in domain terms (e.g. "mapping of basin ID to forcing timeseries" instead of "Dictionary of forcing timeseries data objects").

For functions with multiple or non-obvious arguments, document parameters with an `Inputs:` line followed by a bullet list as the detected package baseline (e.g. `Inputs:\n\n - \`name\` :: description [unit if applicable]`). A `# Arguments` section is an acceptable alternative for new code.

For non-trivial public functions a `# Examples` section containing a `jldoctest` block is aspirational — add one only when the user requests it or when the function is simple enough to keep the doctest perpetually green. When added, use `julia> ` prompts and include expected output so Documenter.jl can run the test.

In every `jldoctest` block, each `julia> ` prompt must be separated from the next by a blank line. Documenter.jl rejects blocks where two `julia> ` lines appear consecutively without an intervening blank line. If a statement produces no output, add a semicolon and a blank line before the next prompt.
If the doctest uses any name from the package, the first statement must be `julia> using <PackageName>` (followed by a blank line). Do not assume the package is already in scope.
`$(TYPEDSIGNATURES)` is always the first line of a function docstring and is the sole source of the signature — never also write a manual indented signature above it.
formatting.example:
## Before (MyProcess has no docstring; fields have inline comments with type-in-brackets anti-pattern)
using DocStringExtensions
struct MyProcess <: Process "Ensemble size [Int]" # BAD: [Int] is the type, not a unit N_ens::Int "Covariance inflation factor" inflation::Float64 end
## After (struct-level docstring added; field comments are semantic, no redundant type in brackets)
""" MyProcess <: Process
Ensemble Kalman update variant using my algorithm.
# Fields
$(TYPEDFIELDS) """ struct MyProcess <: Process "Number of ensemble members" # GOOD: semantic description, no redundant type N_ens::Int "Multiplicative covariance inflation factor" inflation::Float64 end
## Before (function with only a bare macro line)
""" $(TYPEDSIGNATURES) """ function update_ensemble!(ekp::EnsembleKalmanProcess, g::AbstractMatrix; kwargs...) ... end
## After (prose + Inputs: list; $(TYPEDSIGNATURES) stays first as the sole signature source)
""" $(TYPEDSIGNATURES)
Update the ensemble stored in `ekp` using the forward-model output matrix `g`.
Inputs:
- `ekp` :: EnsembleKalmanProcess holding the current ensemble state - `g` :: Forward-model outputs (size: `N_obs × N_ens`)
""" function update_ensemble!(ekp::EnsembleKalmanProcess, g::AbstractMatrix; kwargs...) ... end
